gulliver2018.author: Gulliver, W.P. and colleagues
gulliver2018.title: Review of the Efficacy and Safety of Topical Mahonia aquifolium for the Treatment of Psoriasis and Atopic Dermatitis
gulliver2018.journal: Journal of Clinical and Aesthetic Dermatology

herbalreality.author: Herbal Reality
herbalreality.title: Oregon Grape Root (Berberis aquifolium): Benefits, Uses, Safety

actions.action: Topical treatment of mild-to-moderate psoriasis (reduces plaque severity)
Topical for atopic dermatitis (eczema) and skin irritation
Anti-inflammatory and antiproliferative (slows the skin-cell overgrowth of psoriasis)
Antimicrobial (berberine) - supports skin and mucosal infection

constituents.name: Isoquinoline alkaloids (berberine, berbamine, palmatine, jatrorrhizine, magnoflorine)
constituents.note: Berberine is the main anti-psoriatic and antimicrobial constituent.

contraindications.note: Avoid internal use in pregnancy and breastfeeding: like goldenseal, the berberine it contains can cross the placenta and into milk and worsen newborn jaundice (kernicterus risk).

Berberine impairs the metabolism of some medicines - for example it raises blood levels of cyclosporine, risking toxicity - so use caution with prescription drugs. It is used mainly topically for skin conditions.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific and general literature (J Clin Aesthet Dermatol review of topical Mahonia for psoriasis/eczema; Herbal Reality monograph). Mahonia aquifolium = Berberis aquifolium. Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (psoriasis, eczema, skin-irritation, infection); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

bahramsoltani2024.author: Bahramsoltani, R., Ahmadian, R., Daglia, M. and Rahimi, R.
bahramsoltani2024.title: Petroselinum crispum (Mill.) Fuss (Parsley): An Updated Review of the Traditional Uses, Phytochemistry, and Pharmacology
bahramsoltani2024.journal: Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry

actions.action: Diuretic - reduces fluid retention/swelling and supports urinary and kidney function (traditionally used for urinary stones)
Carminative digestive and stomach tonic (bloating, indigestion)
Breath freshener (chlorophyll-rich; traditional for bad breath)

constituents.name: Essential oil (apiole and myristicin)
constituents.note: Aromatic constituents that drive the diuretic action but are toxic in large amounts.

contraindications.note: Culinary amounts are safe, but medicinal/seed doses and the essential oil (apiole, myristicin) can be toxic and stimulate the uterus - AVOID medicinal doses of parsley in pregnancy.
Its diuretic action means caution alongside diuretic medicines; the furanocoumarins can cause mild photosensitivity, and concentrated use should be avoided in active kidney disease.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific literature (J Agric Food Chem updated review of parsley). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (swelling, kidney-support, bloating, bad-breath); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

janda2020.author: Janda, K., Wojtkowska, K., Jakubczyk, K., Antoniewicz, J. and Skonieczna-Zydecka, K.
janda2020.title: Passiflora incarnata in Neuropsychiatric Disorders - A Systematic Review

ema.author: European Medicines Agency (HMPC)
ema.title: European Union herbal monograph on Passiflora incarnata L., herba (Passiflorae herba)

actions.action: Anxiolytic for anxiety and nervous tension (reduces anxiety in clinical trials)
Sleep support for insomnia (improves sleep quality)

constituents.name: Flavonoids (vitexin, isovitexin, chrysin)
constituents.note: The principal calming constituents, thought to act on GABA pathways.

constituents.name: Trace harman-type alkaloids
constituents.note: Minor constituents that may contribute to the sedative effect.

contraindications.note: Generally well tolerated, with no serious adverse effects reported in trials. Its sedative effect can add to that of alcohol, sedatives and sleep medicines, so avoid combining and do not use before driving.
Use in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not recommended.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific and regulatory literature (Nutrients systematic review; EMA HMPC monograph for Passiflorae herba). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (anxiety, insomnia, stress, nervous-tension); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

careddu2018.author: Careddu, D. and Pettenazzo, A.
careddu2018.title: Pelargonium sidoides extract EPs 7630: a review of its clinical efficacy and safety for treating acute respiratory tract infections in children

conrad2007.author: Conrad, A., Kolodziej, H. and Schulz, V.
conrad2007.title: Pelargonium sidoides-extract (EPs 7630): registration confirms efficacy and safety

actions.action: Acute bronchitis - reduces symptom severity and shortens illness
Common cold and acute respiratory tract infection

constituents.name: Highly oxygenated coumarins (umckalin)
constituents.note: Characteristic root constituents with antibacterial activity.

constituents.name: Polyphenols and proanthocyanidins (gallic acid derivatives)
constituents.note: Contribute to the antiviral and immunomodulatory effects.

contraindications.note: Generally well tolerated; the main effects are mild gastrointestinal upset or, rarely, an allergic skin reaction. Rare reports of liver injury mean you should stop and seek advice if signs of liver problems occur.
The coumarin content gives a theoretical bleeding risk, so use caution with anticoagulant medicines; use in pregnancy and breastfeeding is not recommended.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from scientific literature (Int J Gen Med and Wien Med Wochenschr reviews of the standardised root extract EPs 7630). Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (bronchitis, cold, sore-throat, sinusitis); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.

actions.action: Traditional expectorant - helps clear phlegm in chest conditions, bronchitis and pleurisy
Diaphoretic - promotes sweating to support feverish illness

constituents.name: Cardiac glycosides (cardenolides such as asclepin, calactin and calotropin)
constituents.note: Heart-active compounds that make dosing and safety critical.

contraindications.note: Contains cardiac glycosides: do NOT use in pregnancy, breastfeeding or infancy, in anyone with a heart condition, or alongside cardiac glycoside medicines (e.g. digoxin).
Large doses are emetic and purgative (cause vomiting and diarrhoea). There is very little modern safety or efficacy data, so use only under the guidance of a qualified practitioner.

internal_notes: Enriched 2026-06-12 from general herbal literature (traditional monographs). Record status set to needs-review because Asclepias tuberosa contains cardiac glycosides and has essentially no modern clinical evidence. Originally a skeleton imported from the omniasana.bio Symptom-to-Plant Lookup tool (bronchitis, cough, fever); associations retained and now part-mapped and cited.
